content.text: The Transformer (Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need", 2017) replaced recurrence with pure self-attention and became the universal architecture of deep learning. Within two years it had superseded LSTMs in NLP, within four it dominated computer vision, speech, protein structure prediction, and code generation. The key insight was not just that attention works — Bahdanau had shown that — but that you could build an entire network from attention blocks, remove the sequential bottleneck entirely, and unlock the GPU parallelism needed to scale to billions of parameters.
Scaled Dot-Product Attention
The core operation: each token is projected to query q, key k, and value v vectors. Attention weights are dot products between queries and keys, scaled by √d_k to prevent saturation of the softmax:
Multi-head attention runs h independent attention operations in parallel on projected subspaces, then concatenates and projects the results:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math
from torch import Tensor


class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Multi-head scaled dot-product attention (Vaswani et al., 2017).
    Projects Q, K, V into h heads of dimension d_k = d_model / h,
    computes attention independently per head, then merges.
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model: int = 512, n_heads: int = 8, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % n_heads == 0
        self.d_k    = d_model // n_heads
        self.n_heads = n_heads
        # Fused Q, K, V projection (3× d_model output)
        self.qkv_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, 3 * d_model, bias=False)
        self.out_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.dropout  = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: Tensor, mask: Tensor | None = None) -> Tensor:
        """
        Self-attention: Q = K = V = x.
        x: (B, T, d_model)  mask: (B, 1, T, T) boolean, True = masked out
        """
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        # Project and split into h heads
        q, k, v = self.qkv_proj(x).chunk(3, dim=-1)             # each (B, T, d_model)
        def split_heads(t):
            return t.view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, h, T, d_k)
        q, k, v = split_heads(q), split_heads(k), split_heads(v)

        # Scaled dot-product attention
        scale = math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        scores = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / scale               # (B, h, T, T)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))
        weights = self.dropout(F.softmax(scores, dim=-1))
        attended = weights @ v                                    # (B, h, T, d_k)

        # Merge heads and project
        out = attended.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, -1)  # (B, T, d_model)
        return self.out_proj(out)


class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    """Pre-norm Transformer block: LayerNorm -> Attention -> residual, LayerNorm -> FFN -> residual."""
    def __init__(self, d_model: int = 512, n_heads: int = 8, d_ff: int = 2048):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.attn  = MultiHeadAttention(d_model, n_heads)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.ffn   = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff), nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model),
        )

    def forward(self, x: Tensor, mask: Tensor | None = None) -> Tensor:
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x), mask)   # residual + attention
        x = x + self.ffn(self.norm2(x))           # residual + FFN
        return x
From Transformer to BERT and GPT (2018)
The original Transformer was an encoder-decoder for translation. Two families of models quickly diverged, each using only half the architecture:
BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) — encoder only.

Pre-trained on Masked Language Modelling (MLM): randomly mask 15% of tokens, predict them from bidirectional context. BERT learns rich contextual representations used for classification, NER, and QA via fine-tuning. Bidirectional attention sees both left and right context, making it unsuitable for generation but excellent for understanding.
GPT (Radford et al., 2018) — decoder only.

Pre-trained on causal language modelling: predict the next token from left context only, using a causal (lower-triangular) attention mask. GPT showed that large-scale unsupervised pre-training + fine-tuning outperformed task-specific models. GPT-2 (2019) scaled this to 1.5B parameters and demonstrated zero-shot task transfer — the model could summarise, translate, and answer questions from prompts alone, without any fine-tuning.
Scaling Laws and the GPT-3 Moment (2020)
Kaplan et al. (2020) discovered that language model loss follows smooth power laws in model size N, dataset size D, and compute C — the neural scaling laws:

attrs.latex: L(N) \propto N^{-\alpha_N}, \quad L(D) \propto D^{-\alpha_D}, \quad L(C) \propto C^{-\alpha_C}

content.text: This was transformative: loss is predictable before training. Given a compute budget, you can calculate the optimal model size and token count (Chinchilla laws, 2022: train a model with roughly 20 tokens per parameter). GPT-3 (175B parameters, 2020) demonstrated few-shot in-context learning at a scale that made it practically useful — the model could be adapted to new tasks just by showing examples in the prompt.
The Alignment Turn: InstructGPT and RLHF (2022)

Raw GPT-3 was powerful but unpredictable — it could continue a prompt in unexpected, harmful, or unhelpful directions. InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022) introduced Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) to align the model's outputs with human preferences:
Supervised fine-tuning (SFT): fine-tune GPT-3 on human-written demonstrations of desired behaviour.
Reward model: train a classifier on human preference rankings of model outputs (A is better than B).
RL fine-tuning: update the SFT model with PPO, using the reward model as the reward signal and a KL penalty to prevent the policy drifting too far from the SFT model.

InstructGPT (1.3B) was preferred by human raters over GPT-3 (175B) — RLHF alignment was more important than raw scale for practical usefulness. ChatGPT (November 2022) applied the same technique to GPT-3.5 and reached 100 million users in two months.
Transformer Revolution Timeline
Year
Model / work
Key contribution
Transformer
Self-attention replaces recurrence; full parallelism over sequence
BERT / GPT
Pre-train + fine-tune paradigm; encoder vs decoder split
Zero-shot task transfer; coherent multi-paragraph generation
GPT-3 (175B) + scaling laws
Few-shot in-context learning; predictable power-law scaling
InstructGPT / ChatGPT
RLHF alignment; 100M users in 2 months; consumer AI inflection point
GPT-4 / Claude / Gemini
Multimodal inputs; long context (100k+ tokens); tool use

Mental model: the Transformer revolution happened in three waves. First (2017–2019): architecture — replace RNNs with attention, unlock GPU parallelism, scale to hundreds of millions of parameters. Second (2020–2021): scale — power laws show that bigger is predictably better; GPT-3 demonstrates emergent in-context learning. Third (2022–present): alignment — scale alone is not sufficient; RLHF tunes raw capability into reliably helpful behaviour. Each wave required a different insight, and each built directly on the previous one.
Key Takeaways
The Transformer replaced recurrence with scaled dot-product self-attention, enabling full sequence-level parallelism during training and O(1) dependency path length between any two tokens.
BERT (encoder, MLM) and GPT (decoder, causal LM) split the Transformer into two paradigms: understanding via bidirectional context vs generation via left-to-right prediction.

Neural scaling laws (Kaplan 2020, Chinchilla 2022) showed that loss follows predictable power laws in N, D, and C — making it possible to forecast model quality before training and compute optimal N/D allocations.
GPT-3's few-shot in-context learning showed that a single model could perform dozens of NLP tasks from examples in the prompt — no gradient updates, no task-specific fine-tuning.

RLHF (InstructGPT/ChatGPT) added a preference-alignment layer on top of pre-training; a 1.3B RLHF model outperformed raw GPT-3 (175B) on human evaluations, showing alignment matters more than scale for practical utility.
